Back to Content Agency PathPrevious lessonNext lesson
Build proof fast without lying

PURPOSE OF THIS LESSON

No proof = no trust. And in a content agency, trust is the sale.

Most beginners either:

  • have zero portfolio and can’t close clients, or
  • fake results and destroy credibility long-term, or
  • take low-paying clients just to “get experience” and get trapped.

Operators build proof fast, ethically, and strategically. Your goal is not to look famous. Your goal is to show:

  1. you can produce quality content
  2. you can produce it consistently
  3. you can produce it for a specific business type
  4. you understand hooks, structure, and conversions

This lesson gives you an operator-grade “portfolio fast” system: spec work, pilot offers, proof assets, case study structure, and how to build a portfolio that closes clients without exaggeration.

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of Lesson 3, the student will be able to:

  1. Build a portfolio in 7–14 days using ethical proof methods.
  2. Create spec samples that look real without claiming fake clients or results.
  3. Run a “pilot package” to get real client work and testimonials quickly.
  4. Build a proof library (before/after content, hooks, scripts, edits, captions).
  5. Create case studies that sell even without massI’ve view counts.
  6. Avoid common portfolio traps: lying, free work forever, and random samples with no positioning.

SECTION 1: THE OPERATOR PROOF PRINCIPLE

Proof is not just “a reel.” Proof is evidence that you can deliver results and consistency.

Operator rule: Your portfolio should answer: “Can you do this for me?”

Clients want to see:

  • your editing style and quality
  • your understanding of hooks
  • your ability to match a brand voice
  • your workflow reliability (batches, deadlines)
  • your professionalism (clear deliverables, revisions, organization)

You don’t need millions of views. You need credible proof assets.

SECTION 2: THE 4 TYPES OF PORTFOLIO PROOF (ETHICAL OPTIONS)

You can build proof using four ethical methods:

  1. Spec work (no client)
    You create “as-if” content using public footage or your own footage.
    Key rule: you do not claI’m it was for a paying client.
  2. Pilot package (discounted but paid)
    You sell a short trial package (7–14 days) to a real business at a lower price in exchange for permission to use content and a testimonial.
  3. Your own brand as the client
    You post content using your own account to prove you can write hooks, edit, and grow a content system. This is underrated because it proves consistency.
  4. White-label editing for another agency
    You do work as a subcontractor (sometimes) and can often still use parts of the work privately as “sample edits” if permitted (depends on agreement). Don’t assume permission—get it in writing.

Operator rule: you never fabricate clients or results. That’s short-term gain, long-term collapse.

SECTION 3: THE PORTFOLIO GOAL (WHAT YOU NEED TO CLOSE CLIENTS)

Your portfolio does not need 100 videos. It needs a small set of strong examples:

Minimum portfolio kit (starter):

  • 12–20 short-form edits (your best work)
  • 3 “style lanes” (three different editing styles or niches)
  • 3 caption examples per style lane
  • 1–3 mini case studies (even if results are modest)
  • 1 simple proof page (Google DrI’ve folder, Notion page, or landing page)

Operator rule: your portfolio is a sales tool. Build what sells.

SECTION 4: CHOOSE YOUR “PORTFOLIO LANE” (POSITIONING THROUGH PROOF)

Don’t build random samples. Build samples that target who you want to get paid by.

Pick 1–2 lanes to start:
Lane A: Local services

  • medspa, roofing, pressure washing, real estate
  • Content type: before/after, authority clips, offers

Lane B: Coaches/consultants
Content type: talking head, frameworks, story-driven clips

Lane C: Ecommerce brands
Content type: UGC-style edits, product demos, fast hooks

Operator rule: your samples should match your target clients so they can visualize hiring you.

SECTION 5: HOW TO CREATE SPEC WORK THAT ACTUALLY CLOSES

Spec work fails when it looks like “random edits.” Spec work wins when it looks like a client-ready deliverable.

Spec work rules:

  1. Use clear branding placeholders
    • “Sample edit (spec)” watermark or label on the page
    • don’t use copyrighted logos as if you’re the brand
    • you can say: “Concept for a medspa” without naming the business
  2. Use realistic hooks and offers
    • include a CTA like “Book a consult” or “Free estimate”
    • match the tone of that niche
  3. Create 3 versions of the same clip
    • Version 1: bold captions + jump cuts
    • Version 2: cleaner premium style
    • Version 3: heavy hook style (fast pace)
    • This shows range and testing mindset.
  4. Show the full package
    • video + caption + hashtags + thumbnail frame (optional)

Operator rule: spec work should demonstrate your system, not just your taste.

SECTION 6: THE “12-CLIP PORTFOLIO SPRINT” (7–10 DAY PLAN)

This is a proven portfolio builder.

Day 1: pick 2 niches and 6 hooks each (12 total)
Day 2: gather footage (public clips, stock, or self-filmed)
Day 3–4: edit 6 clips (lane A)
Day 5–6: edit 6 clips (lane B)
Day 7: write captions for all 12
Day 8: organize portfolio page + export in correct format
Day 9–10: optional revisions, polish, and create 3 case study slides

Operator rule: finish beats perfect. A clean portfolio beats an unfinished “idea.”

SECTION 7: THE PILOT PACKAGE (FASTEST WAY TO GET REAL CLIENT PROOF)

A pilot package is how you get real work without giving away your time for free.

Pilot package principles:

  • short duration (7–14 days)
  • limited deliverables
  • paid (even if discounted)
  • clear terms: you get permission to use for portfolio and a testimonial if satisfied

Example pilot packages:
Pilot A: “10 shorts in 10 days”
Pilot B: “2 weekly batches (6 shorts total)”
Pilot C: “Content refresh pack: 8 edits + caption set”

What you get in exchange:

  • a testimonial
  • permission to show the work publicly
  • a case study with before/after (output and workflow improvements)

Operator rule: free work attracts low-quality clients. Paid pilots attract real buyers.

SECTION 8: HOW TO PICK PILOT CLIENTS (DON’T CHOOSE BROKE PEOPLE)

Choose pilot clients who:

  • already make money
  • already value marketing
  • have existing footage or can film easily
  • can become a retainer after the pilot
  • are in your niche lane

Avoid:

  • “I’m just starting” with no offer
  • clients who can’t send footage
  • clients who negotiate endlessly before paying

Operator rule: a pilot is a sales strategy, not charity.

SECTION 9: THE “BEFORE/AFTER” IN CONTENT AGENCY PROOF

You’re not pressure washing driveways, but you can still show before/after.

Content agency before/after examples:

  • before: their old video with weak hook
  • after: your re-edited version with strong hook and captions
  • before: inconsistent posting
  • after: weekly batch and schedule
  • before: messy brand voice
  • after: consistent style guide and caption voice

You can show:

  • hook rewrite comparisons
  • caption rewrite comparisons
  • thumbnail frame improvements
  • pacing improvements

Operator rule: show transformation in quality and clarity, not fake “results.”

SECTION 10: CASE STUDIES THAT SELL WITHOUT “VIRAL” NUMBERS

A case study doesn’t need a million views. It needs a story that proves competence.

Simple case study structure:

  1. Client type (without exposing private info if not allowed)
  2. Goal (what they wanted)
  3. Problem (what was wrong before)
  4. Solution (your workflow + deliverables)
  5. Output proof (examples, screenshots, consistency)
  6. Early signals (inquiries, comments, saves, improved watch time) if available
  7. Next steps (what you’re doing next month)

Even without results, you can show:

  • consistent delivery
  • improved quality
  • professional workflow
  • client satisfaction

Operator rule: you sell professionalism and reliability, not just views.

SECTION 11: TESTIMONIALS (HOW TO GET THEM FAST)

Testimonials convert more than reels sometimes.

How to request:

  • ask after you deliver a strong batch
  • make it easy: gI’ve them a prompt

Testimonial prompt template:
“Can you write 2–3 sentences about:

  1. what we delivered
  2. how it made your life easier
  3. what you liked about the quality and speed?”

Operator rule: don’t ask vaguely. Ask with a template.

SECTION 12: BUILD YOUR PORTFOLIO PAGE (CLEAN AND SIMPLE)

You need a place to send prospects.

Simple portfolio page options:

  • Notion page
  • Google DrI’ve folder with organized subfolders
  • a single-page website
  • a PDF deck (short)

What it must include:

  • your offer lanes (who you serve)
  • 12–20 sample videos (grouped by lane)
  • caption examples
  • short case studies
  • testimonial(s) if available
  • how to contact you

Operator rule: one link that sells. No scattered chaos.

SECTION 13: PROOF LIBRARY ORGANIZATION (SO YOU DON’T WASTE TIME)

Folder structure example:

/Portfolio
/Lane_A_LocalServices
/Edits
/Captions
/Case_Studies
/Lane_B_Coaches
/Edits
/Captions
/Case_Studies
/Testimonials
/Offer_Packages

Operator rule: organization is part of professionalism.

SECTION 14: PORTFOLIO TRAPS (WHAT NOT TO DO)

Trap 1: Lying about results or clients
Fix: label spec work clearly; use real client permission only.

Trap 2: Free work forever
Fix: pilots are paid and time-boxed.

Trap 3: Random samples with no niche
Fix: build lanes tied to target clients.

Trap 4: Over-editing your portfolio and never shipping
Fix: sprint, publish, improve later.

Trap 5: Showing only “cool edits” with no business relevance
Fix: show hooks, captions, CTAs, and offer alignment.

Operator rule: portfolio exists to close paying clients.

SECTION 15: ASSIGNMENTS

Assignment 1: Pick your 2 portfolio lanes
Write them down and define the client type.

Assignment 2: Run the 12-clip sprint
Create 12 spec edits (6 per lane) + captions.

Assignment 3: Build your portfolio page
One link with organized samples, captions, and a short intro.

Assignment 4: Launch a pilot offer
Write your pilot package and send it to 20 prospects.

Assignment 5: Create 2 mini case studies
Even if it’s spec work: show “before/after” improvements (hook/caption/style).

SECTION 16: QUIZ

  1. What are the four ethical ways to build portfolio proof fast?
  2. Why is lying about results a business killer?
  3. What is the minimum portfolio kit that closes clients?
  4. What are “portfolio lanes” and why do they matter?
  5. What is a pilot package and why is it better than free work?
  6. How do you build case studies without viral numbers?
  7. What must your portfolio page include?

CLOSING

Proof closes deals. Build it fast and ethically: spec work that looks client-ready, paid pilots that generate testimonials, and a clean portfolio page that makes prospects say, “This is exactly what I need.” That’s how you move from “trying to start an agency” to getting paid like one.

CONTENT AGENCY PATH
LESSON 4: Outreach System
Operator Training Flow
Study it. Execute it. Save proof.
This lesson is now structured like a real training block so members know exactly what to do after reading. Use the checklist, save proof, take the quiz, and move to the next rep.
Outcome

CONTENT AGENCY PATH · LESSON 4: Outreach System

Daily outbound system and lead tracking

Operator Standard

Do not just read the lesson. Pull the rule, run the rep, and keep proof that the rep happened.

Action Steps
  1. Read the lesson carefully and pull the rules that apply to your business or workflow right now.
  2. Turn those rules into one concrete action you can complete this week.
  3. Use the assignment, quick start, or deliverable section as the work product for this lesson.
  4. Review mistakes, update your system, and then move forward.
Proof to Save
  • One screenshot, document, checklist, quote, plan, or other real artifact from the work you completed.
  • Your notes on what worked, what failed, and what you will change on the next rep.
  • A quiz score or completion mark so you can track progress inside the member area.
Execution Checklist
Take quiz
Progress saves to your member account when you are signed in.
Next Step

Once you finish the action, save the proof, and pass your quiz, move forward instead of re-reading the same lesson forever.

Back to Content Agency PathPrevious lessonNext lesson
Daily outbound system and lead tracking

PURPOSE OF THIS LESSON

Outreach is the engine. If you don’t control outreach, you don’t control income.

Most new agencies fail because they do random outreach: a few DMs one day, nothing for a week, then panic. Operators run outbound like a sales machine: daily volume, clean targeting, tight messaging, tracking, follow-up, and weekly review.

This lesson gives you a complete outreach system you can run from day one:

  • who to contact and where to find them
  • how many messages per day and what to track
  • how to structure your outreach so it compounds
  • how to follow up without being annoying
  • how to book calls consistently and fill a pipeline

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of Lesson 4, the student will be able to:

  1. Build a daily outreach routine that is realistic and repeatable.
  2. Create a prospect list and targeting rules (who to contact, who to skip).
  3. Use a tracking system to manage leads, follow-ups, and outcomes.
  4. Run a follow-up cadence that converts “maybe” into booked calls.
  5. Choose outreach channels (DM, email, LinkedIn, cold calls) and use them correctly.
  6. Measure outreach performance with key metrics and improve weekly.
  7. Avoid the outreach mistakes that destroy deliverability and reputation.

SECTION 1: THE OPERATOR OUTREACH PRINCIPLE

No outreach = no pipeline.
No pipeline = you accept bad clients.
Bad clients = burnout.

Operator rule: outreach happens daily, even when you’re busy.

Outbound is a numbers game, but it’s not spam. It’s targeted volume with good positioning.

SECTION 2: YOUR OUTREACH GOAL (WHAT YOU’RE ACTUALLY TRYING TO GET)

Outreach doesn’t need to “sell the whole service.” Outreach only needs to get the next step:

  • a reply
  • a short call
  • permission to send your portfolio link
  • a meeting booked

Operator rule: outreach is about getting a conversation, not closing in DMs.

SECTION 3: TARGETING (WHO TO CONTACT FIRST)

Start where your service has obvious value and the buyer can pay.

Target criteria (ideal prospects):

  • they have a real business offer (services/products)
  • they post inconsistently or their content is weak (you can improve it)
  • they can benefit from more attention/leads
  • they have signs of revenue (reviews, busy schedule, actI’ve offers)
  • they are visible and reachable (IG/TikTok/LinkedIn/email listed)

Prospect categories that work well:

  • local service businesses (medspa, roofing, real estate, legal, home services)
  • coaches/consultants (already selling an offer)
  • ecommerce brands (consistent products)
  • podcasts/YouTube channels (repurposing gold)
  • agencies that might white-label editing

Operator rule: do not start with broke creators who want “growth hacks.”

SECTION 4: WHERE TO FIND LEADS (PRACTICAL SOURCES)

You need lead sources that let you build a list daily.

Lead sources:

  1. Instagram
    • search niche hashtags + location tags
    • find businesses posting low-quality reels or inconsistent content
  2. TikTok
    • local businesses and niche creators
    • look for people posting but editing is weak or inconsistent
  3. Google Maps / Google Business Profile
    • search “medspa,” “roofing,” “pressure washing,” etc.
    • find businesses with websites and emails
    • check if their socials are weak or inactive
  4. LinkedIn
    • B2B founders, consultants, agencies
    • strong for corporate-style content repurposing
  5. YouTube / Podcasts
    • channels with long content you can repurpose into shorts
    • easiest value proposition: “I’ll turn your long videos into shorts.”

Operator rule: build lists from platforms where your buyer already lives.

SECTION 5: THE “ICP” QUICK FILTER (WHO TO SKIP)

You save time by filtering out prospects that won’t buy.

Skip if:

  • no offer / no service listed
  • no website / no contact path
  • clearly broke brand (no activity, no clients, no proof)
  • they already have high-quality consistent content (harder to pitch)
  • they argue in comments or show unstable behavior publicly
  • they’re in a niche you can’t deliver for (for now)

Operator rule: you don’t need everyone. You need buyers.

SECTION 6: OUTREACH CHANNELS (WHICH ONE WHEN)

Use the channel that matches the prospect.

DM outreach (IG/TikTok)
Best for: local businesses, creators, coaches
Pros: fast, casual
Cons: can be noisy; requires follow-up

Email outreach
Best for: established businesses, agencies, B2B
Pros: professional, scalable
Cons: requires better copy and deliverability discipline

LinkedIn outreach
Best for: consultants, agencies, B2B founders
Pros: business context, higher trust
Cons: slower

Cold calls (optional, advanced for agencies)
Best for: local services if you’re comfortable
Pros: fast decisions
Cons: more rejection; requires confidence

Operator rule: start with 1–2 channels and run them daily. Don’t scatter.

SECTION 7: DAILY OUTREACH VOLUME (WHAT TO DO IN REAL LIFE)

You need volume that you can sustain.

Beginner target (solo):

  • 20–50 outbound messages per day
  • or
  • 10–20 emails + 10–20 DMs per day

If you can’t do that daily, do smaller volume consistently:

  • 10 per day, 6 days/week = 60 touches/week (enough to start pipeline)

Operator rule: consistent daily volume beats one “big grind day” per month.

SECTION 8: OUTREACH SEQUENCE (THE SYSTEM THAT COMPOUNDS)

Your outreach must include follow-up.

Basic 4-touch sequence:
Touch 1: initial message
Touch 2: follow-up 2 days later
Touch 3: follow-up 5–7 days later
Touch 4: “close the loop” message 10–14 days later

Why follow-up matters:
Most buyers ignore the first message. They respond when they have time. Your follow-up puts you back on top.

Operator rule: follow-up is where deals are made.

SECTION 9: LEAD TRACKING (THE PIPELINE YOU MUST HAVE)

If you don’t track, you will:

  • forget follow-ups
  • double-message people randomly
  • lose leads you could have closed
  • never learn what works

Minimum tracking fields:

  • prospect name
  • niche
  • platform/contact info
  • date contacted
  • status (no response, replied, booked call, closed, not fit)
  • next follow-up date
  • notes (what they said, what they need)
  • offer tier pitched

Statuses (simple pipeline):

  1. New lead
  2. Contacted
  3. Replied
  4. Call booked
  5. Proposal sent
  6. Closed-won
  7. Closed-lost
  8. Not fit

Operator rule: pipeline tracking is the difference between a business and guessing.

SECTION 10: THE OUTREACH CALENDAR (DAILY + WEEKLY ROUTINE)

Daily routine (45–90 minutes):

  1. build 10–20 new leads
  2. send outreach messages
  3. do follow-ups from your tracker
  4. reply to responses and book calls
  5. log everything

Weekly routine (30 minutes):

  1. review metrics (see Section 11)
  2. identify which niche/channel performs best
  3. refine target list and messaging
  4. plan next week’s outreach blocks

Operator rule: outbound becomes easy when scheduled.

SECTION 11: METRICS (WHAT TO MEASURE SO YOU IMPROVE)

Track weekly:

  • # new leads added
  • # outbound messages sent
  • # replies
  • reply rate = replies / messages
  • # calls booked
  • booking rate = calls booked / replies
  • # deals closed
  • close rate = closed / calls
  • average deal value

Use metrics to fix problems:

  • low replies → targeting/message weak
  • replies but no calls → CTA weak, you’re not steering to call
  • calls but no closes → offer/pricing/qualification weak

Operator rule: numbers reveal the bottleneck.

SECTION 12: HOW TO AVOID SPAM FLAGS AND BURNOUT

Don’t spray and pray. Protect your accounts and reputation.

Rules:

  • personalize lightly (1–2 details)
  • don’t send giant paragraphs
  • avoid links in first message if deliverability is an issue (send link after interest)
  • don’t message the same person from multiple accounts aggressively
  • keep a professional tone
  • stop if someone says no (respect)

Operator rule: targeted outreach builds reputation. Spam destroys it.

SECTION 13: THE “OPERATOR LIST BUILDING” METHOD (FAST AND CLEAN)

Here’s a simple method that works:

  1. Choose one niche + one location (or one niche online)
  2. Build a list of 200 prospects
  3. Run the 4-touch sequence over 2–3 weeks
  4. Track responses and refine
  5. Repeat with next 200

Operator rule: list volume is power.

SECTION 14: COMMON OUTREACH FAILURES (AND FIXES)

Failure 1: doing outreach only when you need money
Fix: daily routine.

Failure 2: no tracking
Fix: simple pipeline sheet.

Failure 3: no follow-up
Fix: 4-touch sequence.

Failure 4: targeting bad prospects
Fix: ICP filter and skip list.

Failure 5: trying too many channels
Fix: pick 1–2 channels and dominate them.

Failure 6: selling too hard in first message
Fix: goal is reply and call, not full close.

Operator rule: outreach is a process, not a personality test.

SECTION 15: ASSIGNMENTS

Assignment 1: Choose your outreach channel(s)
Pick 1–2 channels for the next 14 days.

Assignment 2: Build your first 100-lead list
Use your niche and lead sources to create 100 prospects.

Assignment 3: Create your tracking sheet
Add pipeline statuses and follow-up dates.

Assignment 4: Run 7 days of outreach
Send outreach daily and follow the sequence.

Assignment 5: Weekly review
At end of week, compute reply rate and call booking rate and write one improvement.

SECTION 16: QUIZ

  1. What is the goal of outreach (reply, call, conversation) and why?
  2. What makes a good prospect for a content agency?
  3. What are the best lead sources for your niche?
  4. What is the 4-touch outreach sequence and why does it work?
  5. What fields must your lead tracker include?
  6. What metrics do you track weekly and what do they diagnose?
  7. What are the common outreach failures and fixes?

CLOSING

Outreach is your income lever. Run it daily with targeting, tracking, follow-up, and weekly review. You don’t need luck—you need volume and system discipline. Do that, and calls become predictable.

CONTENT AGENCY PATH
LESSON 5: Scripts That Get Replies
Operator Training Flow
Study it. Execute it. Save proof.
This lesson is now structured like a real training block so members know exactly what to do after reading. Use the checklist, save proof, take the quiz, and move to the next rep.
Outcome

CONTENT AGENCY PATH · LESSON 5: Scripts That Get Replies

DM/email scripts and follow-up

Operator Standard

Do not just read the lesson. Pull the rule, run the rep, and keep proof that the rep happened.

Action Steps
  1. Read the lesson carefully and pull the rules that apply to your business or workflow right now.
  2. Turn those rules into one concrete action you can complete this week.
  3. Use the assignment, quick start, or deliverable section as the work product for this lesson.
  4. Review mistakes, update your system, and then move forward.
Proof to Save
  • One screenshot, document, checklist, quote, plan, or other real artifact from the work you completed.
  • Your notes on what worked, what failed, and what you will change on the next rep.
  • A quiz score or completion mark so you can track progress inside the member area.
Execution Checklist
Take quiz
Progress saves to your member account when you are signed in.
Next Step

Once you finish the action, save the proof, and pass your quiz, move forward instead of re-reading the same lesson forever.

Back to Content Agency PathPrevious lessonNext lesson
DM/email scripts and follow-up

PURPOSE OF THIS LESSON

Outreach works when the message is simple, specific, and low-friction.

Most people write outreach that sounds like:

  • generic (“Hey, I do content…”)
  • needy (“Please gI’ve me a chance…”)
  • spammy (“We guarantee viral growth…”)
  • too long (nobody reads paragraphs in DMs)
  • too early with links and attachments

Operators write scripts that do one job: get a reply. Then they move the conversation to a call or a quick qualification thread.

This lesson gives you a complete script library:

  • DMs for Instagram/TikTok
  • emails for businesses and agencies
  • follow-up messages (4-touch sequence)
  • qualification questions
  • short “send portfolio” messages
  • handling objections without discounting
  • booking a call smoothly

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of Lesson 5, the student will be able to:

  1. Use short scripts that consistently get replies.
  2. Customize scripts with light personalization without wasting time.
  3. Run a follow-up cadence that increases reply rate without being annoying.
  4. Ask qualification questions that filter bad leads fast.
  5. Move from DM/email → booked call efficiently.
  6. Handle common objections professionally and hold pricing power.
  7. Build a personal script pack they can copy/paste daily.

SECTION 1: THE OPERATOR SCRIPT PRINCIPLES

Rule 1: Short wins
If your message is longer than the prospect’s attention span, it’s dead.

Rule 2: Specific wins
“Short-form repurposing for podcasters” beats “content services.”

Rule 3: Low-friction question wins
End with a simple question they can answer in one word.

Rule 4: Respectful confidence wins
No begging, no hype, no desperation.

Rule 5: Proof comes after interest
Don’t throw 5 links at strangers. Offer proof after they respond.

Operator rule: a script is a tool. Use it, track it, improve it.

SECTION 2: THE BEST OUTREACH FORMAT (THE 4-LINE DM)

A high-performing DM often looks like this:

Line 1: Personalize lightly (1 detail)
Line 2: Who you are + what you do (one sentence)
Line 3: Specific value (outcome or deliverable)
Line 4: Simple question (yes/no)

Example skeleton:
“Hey [Name] — saw your [post/topic].
I run short-form edits for [niche] so you can post consistently without spending hours editing.
I can turn your long videos into 12–20 clean shorts/month in your style.
Want me to send 2 sample edits?”

Operator rule: the goal is “yes, send it.”

SECTION 3: PERSONALIZATION (HOW TO DO IT FAST)

Personalization should take 5–10 seconds, not 5 minutes.

Good personalization examples:

  • “Saw your video about [topic]”
  • “Noticed you’re posting 1–2x/week”
  • “Your offer looks strong—content could scale it”
  • “Your edits are solid; consistency would multiply it” (careful—don’t compliment too hard)

Bad personalization:

  • long essays about their life
  • fake compliments
  • mentioning private details

Operator rule: one real detail is enough.

SECTION 4: DM SCRIPTS (INSTAGRAM / TIKTOK)

Use these as copy/paste templates. Replace [brackets].

DM #1: Repurposing angle (best all-around)
“Hey [Name] — saw your [recent video/topic].
I help [niche] turn 1 long video into 10–20 shorts/month so they stay visible daily.
If I made 2 sample cuts from your content, would you want to see them?”

DM #2: Local service business angle
“Hey [Name] — saw your page. Quick one: your services look solid, but the Reels could hit harder.
I run a short-form package for local businesses: 12 posts/month + captions.
Want me to send a sample edit style for your brand?”

DM #3: “You’re posting but…” angle
“Hey [Name] — noticed you’re posting, but it’s not consistent yet.
We help businesses batch content weekly so they don’t fall off.
Want a quick breakdown of what we’d post for you each week?”

DM #4: “Pilot offer” angle
“Hey [Name] — I’m running a short pilot this week: 6 shorts in 7 days (editing + captions).
If you want to test it with your content, I can send details. Interested?”

DM #5: White-label for agencies
“Hey [Name] — I saw you run an agency.
If you ever need overflow short-form editing, I can deliver weekly batches with your style guide.
Want me to send examples + rates?”

Operator rule: keep DMs clean and ask one question.

SECTION 5: EMAIL SCRIPTS (BUSINESSES + AGENCIES)

Email needs a subject line that doesn’t sound like spam.

Subject line options:

  • “Short-form content for [Business Name]”
  • “Quick idea to improve your Reels”
  • “12 shorts/month offer (done-for-you)”
  • “Question about your content”

Email #1: Business repurposing pitch
Subject: “Short-form content for [Business Name]”
Body:
“Hi [Name],
I’m [Your Name]. I help [niche] turn their existing content into consistent short-form posts (Reels/TikTok/Shorts).
If you have long videos or even phone clips, we can turn them into 12–20 shorts/month in a clean brand style—delivered weekly.
Would you be open to me sending 2 sample edits and a simple package?
– [Name]”

Email #2: Local business quick pitch
Subject: “Quick idea for your Reels”
Body:
“Hi [Name],
I noticed your business is strong but your short-form content isn’t posting consistently.
We run a done-for-you short-form package: weekly batch edits + captions so your page stays actI’ve and credible.
If you want, I can send a 1-page offer and examples. Interested?
– [Name]”

Email #3: Agency white-label
Subject: “Overflow editing support”
Body:
“Hi [Name],
If you ever need overflow capacity for short-form editing, I can deliver weekly batches using your style guide (fast turnaround, clean workflow).
Want me to send examples and rates?
– [Name]”

Operator rule: email is still about getting a reply, not selling the whole service.

SECTION 6: FOLLOW-UP SCRIPTS (4-TOUCH SEQUENCE)

Most replies come from follow-up. Keep follow-ups short and polite.

Follow-up #1 (Day 2)
“Hey [Name] — quick bump. Want me to send a couple sample edits so you can see the style?”

Follow-up #2 (Day 5–7)
“Checking in—are you currently posting short-form consistently, or is it on the back burner?”

Follow-up #3 (Day 10–14) “close the loop”
“No worries if now’s not the time. Want me to close this out, or should I send a quick package overview?”

Operator rule: follow-up asks a question. No guilt. No pressure.

SECTION 7: WHEN THEY REPLY (WHAT TO SAY NEXT)

If they reply “yeah” or “send it” you need a clean next step.

Reply #1: Send portfolio (simple)
“Perfect. What’s your main goal right now—more leads, more local visibility, or building authority?
I’ll send 2 samples that match.”

Then send:

  • 1 portfolio link
  • 2 specific sample links (or attach)
  • 1 sentence explaining the package

Reply #2: Ask for content source
“Do you have long videos/podcasts already, or are we starting from raw phone clips?”

Reply #3: Move to call
“Got it. If you want, we can do a quick 10-minute call and I’ll map the best package and weekly cadence for you. Want (A) today at ___ or (B) tomorrow at ___?”

Operator rule: steer to a call as soon as there’s interest.

SECTION 8: QUALIFICATION QUESTIONS (FILTER FAST)

You don’t want to spend time with bad fits.

Ask 3–5 questions max:

  1. “What are you selling (main offer)?”
  2. “How often can you realistically film content?”
  3. “Do you already have footage we can repurpose?”
  4. “Are you looking for weekly posting or monthly batches?”
  5. “Are you trying to grow brand presence or drI’ve direct leads?”

If they can’t answer these, they’re not ready.

Operator rule: no offer, no content strategy. You can’t market nothing.

SECTION 9: OBJECTION HANDLING (WITHOUT DISCOUNTING)

Objection: “How much?” (too early)
“Depends on volume and how hands-on you want us to be. Most clients start at [range] for [deliverables]. If you tell me your goal and how much footage you have, I’ll point you to the right package.”

Objection: “We already have an editor.”
“Perfect. If you ever need overflow capacity or want a second style lane for testing, I can help. Want me to send examples so you have them?”

Objection: “Send pricing.”
“Absolutely. Before I do—are you mainly after more leads or just consistent posting? Pricing depends on volume and turnaround.”

Objection: “We don’t have time to film.”
“That’s common. If you have any long videos, calls, podcasts, or even 20 minutes of phone clips weekly, we can repurpose them into consistent shorts. Do you have anything we can pull from?”

Objection: “Can you guarantee views?”
“No—platform results depend on audience, offer, and consistency. What we guarantee is consistent delivery, strong hooks, and a testing system so content improves month over month.”

Operator rule: protect standards. Don’t promise what you can’t control.

SECTION 10: BOOKING A CALL (THE TWO-OPTION CLOSE)

When they show interest, don’t ask “when are you free.”

Call booking script:
“Cool. Quick 10–15 min call so I can map the best package and cadence.
I can do (A) ___ or (B) ___. Which works?”

Operator rule: two options close faster.

SECTION 11: SCRIPT PACK (WHAT TO SAVE IN YOUR PHONE)

Save these as text shortcuts:

  • DM initial (repurposing)
  • DM follow-up 1
  • DM follow-up 2
  • “send portfolio + ask goal”
  • “ask content source”
  • “book call two options”
  • objection: “price range”
  • objection: “guarantee”
  • “pilot offer”

Operator rule: don’t rewrite messages daily. Use a pack.

SECTION 12: COMMON SCRIPT FAILURES (AND FIXES)

Failure 1: too long
Fix: 4 lines max.

Failure 2: too generic
Fix: mention niche + deliverables.

Failure 3: too salesy
Fix: ask permission: “want me to send samples?”

Failure 4: no question at the end
Fix: every message ends with a simple question.

Failure 5: no follow-up
Fix: 4-touch sequence.

SECTION 13: ASSIGNMENTS

Assignment 1: Build your script pack
Pick 3 DM scripts + 2 follow-ups + 1 email script + 1 call booking script.

Assignment 2: Send 100 messages this week
Track reply rate and identify best script.

Assignment 3: Run follow-ups
Follow up on every non-response using the sequence.

Assignment 4: Practice qualification
Ask the 3–5 questions to every positI’ve reply.

Assignment 5: Book calls
Use the two-option close to book at least 3 calls this week.

SECTION 14: QUIZ

  1. What is the goal of the first message?
  2. What is the 4-line DM structure and why does it work?
  3. How do you personalize without wasting time?
  4. What is the follow-up cadence and why does it matter?
  5. What qualification questions filter bad leads fast?
  6. How do you handle “how much?” without getting stuck?
  7. What is the two-option call booking script?

CLOSING

Scripts are leverage. Keep them short, specific, and question-based. Follow up consistently, qualify quickly, and move interested prospects to a call. Do that daily and your pipeline becomes predictable.

CONTENT AGENCY PATH
LESSON 6: Sales Calls + Closing
Operator Training Flow
Study it. Execute it. Save proof.
This lesson is now structured like a real training block so members know exactly what to do after reading. Use the checklist, save proof, take the quiz, and move to the next rep.
Outcome

CONTENT AGENCY PATH · LESSON 6: Sales Calls + Closing

Discovery, pitch, close, deposits

Operator Standard

Do not just read the lesson. Pull the rule, run the rep, and keep proof that the rep happened.

Action Steps
  1. Read the lesson carefully and pull the rules that apply to your business or workflow right now.
  2. Turn those rules into one concrete action you can complete this week.
  3. Use the assignment, quick start, or deliverable section as the work product for this lesson.
  4. Review mistakes, update your system, and then move forward.
Proof to Save
  • One screenshot, document, checklist, quote, plan, or other real artifact from the work you completed.
  • Your notes on what worked, what failed, and what you will change on the next rep.
  • A quiz score or completion mark so you can track progress inside the member area.
Execution Checklist
Take quiz
Progress saves to your member account when you are signed in.
Next Step

Once you finish the action, save the proof, and pass your quiz, move forward instead of re-reading the same lesson forever.

Back to Content Agency PathPrevious lessonNext lesson
Discovery, pitch, close, deposits

PURPOSE OF THIS LESSON

Your outreach gets replies. The sales call turns interest into money.

Most agencies lose deals on calls because they:

  • talk too much and ask too little
  • pitch features instead of outcomes
  • don’t control the next step
  • quote price with no context
  • let prospects “think about it” forever
  • don’t collect payment upfront, so clients drift or ghost

Operators run sales calls like a system: discovery → diagnose → prescribe → close. You lead the conversation calmly, you keep it outcome-focused, you set expectations, and you close with clear options and clear payment terms.

This lesson gives you a complete call framework, scripts, and closing process.

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of Lesson 6, the student will be able to:

  1. Run a structured discovery call that uncovers the real need.
  2. Position their offer as a solution to a business outcome, not “editing.”
  3. Present packages with tiered options and recommend the best fit.
  4. Handle common objections without discounting or sounding defensive.
  5. Close the call with a clear next step: paid onboarding / deposit / start date.
  6. Set client expectations on inputs, approvals, and turnaround to prevent scope creep.
  7. Use a follow-up plan that closes deals if they don’t commit on the call.

SECTION 1: THE OPERATOR CALL PRINCIPLE

The call is not a conversation. It’s a decision meeting.

Your job:

  • understand the problem
  • show the plan
  • confirm fit
  • collect payment and start

Operator rule: if you don’t control the next step, you don’t close.

SECTION 2: THE SALES CALL STRUCTURE (30 MINUTES, CLEAN)

A simple structure that works:

  1. Frame the call (2 minutes)
  2. Discovery (10–12 minutes)
  3. Diagnosis + gap summary (2–3 minutes)
  4. Prescription (your plan) (5–7 minutes)
  5. Packages + recommendation (5 minutes)
  6. Close + payment + onboarding next step (3–5 minutes)

Operator rule: the client should talk more than you during discovery.

SECTION 3: FRAME THE CALL (SET CONTROL EARLY)

Start with a clear agenda so you run the meeting.

Opening script:
“Appreciate you taking the call. Here’s what we’ll do: I’ll ask a few questions about your offer and content, then I’ll recommend the best package and cadence. If it’s a fit, we’ll pick a start date and get you onboarded today. Sound good?”

Operator rule: the frame makes the close natural, not awkward.

SECTION 4: DISCOVERY QUESTIONS (THE ONES THAT ACTUALLY MATTER)

You need to uncover: offer, audience, capacity, content assets, goals, and constraints.

Core discovery questions:

1. Offer and buyer

  • “What do you sell, and who buys it?”
  • “What’s your average sale value / client value?” (or “what’s a client worth?”)

2. Current content situation

  • “What are you posting now and how often?”
  • “What’s working and what isn’t?”
  • “Do you have long content we can repurpose?”

3. Goal and timeline

  • “What’s the goal for content: leads, authority, consistency, recruiting, brand?”
  • “Any launches or deadlines coming up?”

4. Inputs and capacity

  • “How often can you realistically film?”
  • “Who approves content and how fast can approvals happen?”

5. Constraints

  • “Any brand rules, compliance limits, or topics to avoid?”
  • “Do you want us to post, or just deliver for you to post?”

Operator rule: your offer must match their reality. If they can’t produce inputs, you must design around that.

SECTION 5: DIAGNOSIS (SUMMARIZE THE GAP)

After discovery, you summarize what you heard. This builds trust and positions you as the professional.

Diagnosis script:
“Got it. You’ve got a strong offer, but content is inconsistent, and the edits aren’t structured to hold attention and convert. The gap is: you need a weekly system that turns your footage into consistent shorts with hooks and captions—without you spending hours managing it.”

Then confirm:
“Is that accurate?”

Operator rule: if they agree with the diagnosis, they’ll accept the prescription.

SECTION 6: PRESCRIPTION (YOUR PLAN — SIMPLE, SPECIFIC)

Now you explain your plan at a high level.

Plan script:
“Here’s what I recommend: we run weekly batch delivery. You send [X] raw clips or 1 long video per week. We turn it into [Y] shorts with strong hooks, captions, and formatting in your style. You get a batch every [day], with [revision policy]. We track what performs and tighten the style monthly.”

Keep it short. Don’t overwhelm.

Operator rule: prescription is the “how,” but at a high level. Details belong in onboarding.

SECTION 7: PACKAGES (SHOW 3 OPTIONS AND RECOMMEND ONE)

You present three packages and recommend one based on their goals.

Present like this:
“I’ll gI’ve you three options—most people pick the middle.”

Example structure (generic):
Option 1 (Starter): X/month, standard turnaround
Option 2 (Growth): higher volume + faster cadence + more support
Option 3 (Domination): max volume + priority + posting/reporting

Recommendation script:
“Based on your goal and capacity, I recommend Option 2. It’s enough volume to build consistency and test hooks without overwhelming approvals.”

Operator rule: always recommend. If you don’t, you force them to guess.

SECTION 8: PRICE DELIVERY (HOW TO SAY PRICE WITHOUT FLINCHING)

Price should be delivered calmly, then stop talking.

Price script:
“Option 2 is $___ per month, billed upfront. That includes [deliverables], [cadence], and [revision limit].”

Then pause. Let them react.

Operator rule: the first person to talk after price loses leverage. Pause.

SECTION 9: CLOSE (GET A DECISION AND A START DATE)

If they’re aligned, close cleanly.

Close script:
“If you want to move forward, we can start [date]. The next step is payment for the first month and the onboarding intake form. Once that’s complete, we deliver your first batch within [time]. Want to lock that in?”

Two-option start date close:
“I can onboard you today and start this week, or we can start next week. Which do you prefer?”

Operator rule: close with a decision, not “let me know.”

SECTION 10: DEPOSITS AND PAYMENT TERMS (PROTECT YOUR TIME)

For retainers:

  • monthly upfront billing is standard operator practice
  • “work begins after payment + intake”

For pilots:

  • paid upfront
  • clear deliverables and timeline

Payment discipline rules:

  • late payment pauses production
  • no producing while unpaid
  • no “we’ll pay after you send the videos” arrangements

Operator rule: you’re not an employee. You’re a vendor.

SECTION 11: OBJECTION HANDLING (THE BIG ONES)

Objection 1: “It’s expensive.”
Response:
“I understand. The question is: do you want consistent content production handled so you can stay visible and convert? If you want cheaper, we can reduce volume. If price drops, output drops. Do you want Starter or Growth?”

Objection 2: “We need to think about it.”
Response:
“Totally fair. What do you need to think through—budget, fit, or timing?”
(Answer.)
“If it’s a fit, the next step is simple: we pick a start date and get onboarding done. Do you want to start this week or next week?”

Objection 3: “We already have an editor.”
Response:
“Perfect. If your editor is consistent and hitting the style you want, you may not need us. Where teams use us is overflow + systems: weekly batches, hooks, captions, and a reliable cadence. Do you want an overflow package or should we close it out?”

Objection 4: “Can you guarantee views/leads?”
Response:
“No. Platform performance depends on audience, offer, and consistency. What we guarantee is consistent delivery, strong hook structure, and iteration based on what’s working.”

Objection 5: “We don’t have time to film.”
Response:
“Understood. If you can’t film weekly, we build a repurposing plan from long content or schedule one filming block per month and batch it. Do you have any long videos, podcasts, or calls we can cut?”

Operator rule: objections are usually about clarity, not price. Diagnose and offer options.

SECTION 12: QUALIFICATION (WHEN TO SAY NO)

Not every prospect is a fit. Saying no protects your agency.

Red flags:

  • no offer or unclear business model
  • wants “viral” but refuses to post consistently
  • can’t provide footage and refuses to create it
  • wants unlimited revisions
  • disrespectful communication
  • refuses upfront payment

Disqualify script:
“Based on what you told me, I don’t think we’re the best fit right now. If you get [inputs] in place, we can revisit.”

Operator rule: bad clients cost more than they pay.

SECTION 13: FOLLOW-UP AFTER CALL (IF THEY DON’T CLOSE)

If they don’t close on the call, you need a follow-up sequence.

Same day email/text:

  • recap the plan and recommended package
  • restate deliverables and price
  • include next step link (invoice/onboarding)

Follow-up Day 2:
“Any questions on the package? If you want, we can start [date].”

Follow-up Day 5–7:
“Should I close this out for now, or do you want to lock in a start date?”

Operator rule: follow-up is professional and controlled, not needy.

SECTION 14: THE CALL CHECKLIST (PRINT THIS)

Before call:

  • review their page/content
  • identify 1–2 improvement points
  • have packages and pricing ready
  • have onboarding + payment link ready

During call:

  • frame agenda
  • discovery questions
  • gap summary
  • prescription
  • present 3 options
  • recommend one
  • price + pause
  • close with start date + payment

After call:

  • send recap
  • send invoice/payment link
  • send intake form
  • schedule onboarding (Lesson 7)

Operator rule: calls close when you have next-step assets ready.

SECTION 15: ASSIGNMENTS

Assignment 1: Build your call script
Write your opening frame, discovery questions, gap summary, and close.

Assignment 2: Build your package sheet
One page: 3 options with deliverables and price.

Assignment 3: Practice 5 mock calls
Roleplay with a friend or record yourself.

Assignment 4: Close discipline
On your next 3 real calls, always: recommend a package, propose a start date, and ask for payment.

Assignment 5: Follow-up system
Write your 3-step follow-up and use it on every non-close.

SECTION 16: QUIZ

  1. What is the 6-part structure of a strong sales call?
  2. Why do you frame the call at the beginning?
  3. What discovery questions matter most for a content agency?
  4. What is the diagnosis step and why does it increase closes?
  5. How do you present packages and why should you recommend one?
  6. How do you handle “think about it” without losing control?
  7. What are your payment terms and why do they protect your agency?

CLOSING

Sales calls are a system. Run discovery, diagnose the gap, prescribe a simple plan, present three options, recommend one, and close with a start date and upfront payment. That’s how you turn outreach into predictable revenue without chasing clients.

Scope, brand intake, access, and expectations

Do not just read the lesson. Pull the rule, run the rep, and keep proof that the rep happened.

Action Steps

  2. Turn those rules into one concrete action you can complete this week.
  3. Use the assignment, quick start, or deliverable section as the work product for this lesson.
  4. Review mistakes, update your system, and then move forward.

  • Your notes on what worked, what failed, and what you will change on the next rep.

Back to Content Agency PathPrevious lessonNext lesson
Scope, brand intake, access, and expectations

PURPOSE OF THIS LESSON

Closing a client is not the finish line. Onboarding is where retention is won or lost.

Most agencies lose clients in the first 30 days because onboarding is sloppy: no clear scope, no brand intake, missing access, slow approvals, unclear deadlines, and endless “can you also…” requests. The work becomes chaotic, delivery slips, and the client doubts you.

Operators run onboarding like a checklist. The goal is simple:

  • lock scope
  • gather everything you need
  • set expectations
  • establish workflow rhythm
  • deliver first batch fast

This lesson gives you an operator-grade onboarding SOP you can run for every client.

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of Lesson 7, the student will be able to:

  1. Onboard a client in under 60 minutes of admin time using a checklist.
  2. Collect the right brand intake info to match voice and style fast.
  3. Secure access and assets (logins, folders, brand kit, raw footage) safely.
  4. Set scope, revision rules, approval windows, and delivery cadence clearly.
  5. Prevent common onboarding failures that cause delays and churn.
  6. Build a “first week win” plan so the client sees results quickly.
  7. Create an onboarding packet that makes the agency look professional.

SECTION 1: THE OPERATOR ONBOARDING PRINCIPLE

Onboarding is a risk-control process.

Your job is to eliminate uncertainty:

  • what you’re delivering
  • what the client must provide
  • when deliveries happen
  • how approvals work
  • how revisions work
  • how communication works

Operator rule: if it’s unclear, it becomes a problem later.

SECTION 2: THE ONBOARDING FLOW (THE 7 STEPS)

Here is the standard onboarding flow you will run every time:

  1. Welcome + confirmation of scope
  2. Intake form completed (brand, offer, audience, content goals)
  3. Access + assets collected (folders, logins, footage pipeline)
  4. Style guide and “Do/Don’t” rules established
  5. Workflow rules set (delivery cadence, approvals, revisions)
  6. Content plan for the first 2 weeks
  7. First batch delivered fast (first win)

Operator rule: onboarding is not a chat thread. It’s a sequence.

SECTION 3: STEP 1 — WELCOME MESSAGE (SET THE TONE)

Your welcome message should:

  • confirm what they bought
  • outline what happens next
  • set timeline expectations
  • tell them exactly what you need

Welcome message template:
“Welcome aboard. We’re delivering [X deliverables] per [week/month], with delivery on [days]. Next step is onboarding: please complete the intake form today and upload your content to the folder. Once intake + assets are complete, we deliver your first batch within [time].”

Operator rule: clear timeline reduces client anxiety and texts.

SECTION 4: STEP 2 — SCOPE CONFIRMATION (LOCK THE AGREEMENT)

Before you do any creatI’ve work, confirm scope in writing.

Scope confirmation checklist:

  • deliverables count
  • format and length (9:16, 15–45 seconds, etc.)
  • caption inclusion (yes/no)
  • posting responsibility (agency posts or client posts)
  • revision limit and what counts as a revision
  • turnaround time
  • monthly reporting (yes/no)
  • communication channel and response expectations

Operator rule: scope confirmation prevents “I thought this included…” later.

SECTION 5: STEP 3 — THE INTAKE FORM (WHAT YOU MUST COLLECT)

Your intake form should collect the information that controls creatI’ve decisions.

Intake must include:

A) Business and offer

  • what they sell
  • who they sell to
  • main CTA (book call, buy product, DM keyword, etc.)
  • primary objections they hear

B) Brand voice and style

  • tone (professional, casual, bold, luxury, etc.)
  • words they use and avoid
  • competitors they like/dislike
  • 3–5 example creators/accounts they want to emulate (style references)

C) Content strategy basics

  • 3–5 content pillars (topics you’ll rotate)
  • do they want education, entertainment, proof, lifestyle, behind-the-scenes?
  • any compliance restrictions (medical, legal, finance, etc.)

D) Visual style

  • caption style (bold captions, minimal captions, none)
  • brand colors/fonts (if used)
  • logo usage rules (optional)

E) Logistics

  • approval person and turnaround speed
  • filming schedule (weekly, monthly batch, repurposing only)
  • preferred communication channel (email, Slack, WhatsApp)
  • file delivery preferences

Operator rule: intake is how you prevent endless revisions. If you skip it, you’ll guess—and guessing costs time.

SECTION 6: STEP 4 — ACCESS + ASSETS (GET THE MATERIALS OR YOU CAN’T PRODUCE)

You need a secure, organized asset pipeline.

Access and assets checklist:

  • shared folder (Drive/Dropbox) with subfolders
    • /Raw
    • /Edits_Drafts
    • /Final
    • /Brand_Assets
    • /Captions
  • brand assets (logo, fonts, colors, brand guide if exists)
  • raw footage pipeline (how they upload weekly)
  • any existing long-form content (podcasts, YouTube, webinars)
  • existing captions or brand voice examples
  • posting access (only if you are responsible for posting)
  • permissions for using content in your portfolio (if agreed)

Security rules (operator):

  • never ask for passwords in plain text
  • use official access methods when possible
  • keep client assets separate by folder
  • limit who on your team has access

Operator rule: no assets = no delivery. Get the pipeline locked first.

SECTION 7: STEP 5 — STYLE GUIDE (YOUR QUALITY CONTROL TOOL)

Your style guide prevents inconsistent edits and revision loops.

Minimum style guide includes:

  • hook style (how the first 1–2 seconds should feel)
  • caption format (font, size, placement, emphasis style)
  • pacing rules (jump cuts, dead air removal)
  • b-roll rules (how often, what types)
  • music rules (if used)
  • CTA style (how you end videos)
  • banned topics/words
  • “Do/Don’t” list

Operator rule: a style guide turns creatI’ve into production.

SECTION 8: STEP 6 — APPROVALS + REVISIONS (THE PART THAT SAVES YOUR LIFE)

This is where most agencies lose money badly.

Approval rules to set:

  • client has X hours/days to approve drafts
  • drafts not approved by deadline roll to next batch
  • feedback must be consolidated (one person, one message)
  • feedback must be specific (timestamps, what to change)
  • revision limit is enforced

Revision definition examples:
Counts as a revision:

  • text changes, caption corrections
  • minor trI’m changes
  • CTA adjustment

Does not count as “minor”:
“new angle, new concept, new style” (that’s a re-edit)

Operator rule: revision rules protect delivery schedules and team sanity.

SECTION 9: STEP 7 — DELIVERY CADENCE (MAKE THE WORKFLOW REAL)

Your cadence must be written and consistent.

Examples:

  • “Client uploads raw clips by Sunday night. We deliver drafts Tuesday. Client approves by Wednesday. Finals delivered Thursday.”
  • “Weekly batch delivered every Monday. Approvals due within 48 hours.”

Operator rule: cadence prevents last-minute emergencies.

SECTION 10: THE FIRST 2-WEEK CONTENT PLAN (FAST WIN)

You want a quick win early so the client feels momentum.

First 2-week plan includes:

  • content pillars rotation (ex: education, proof, story, offer)
  • 10–20 hook ideas aligned to their offer
  • which footage you’ll use
  • posting schedule recommendation (if client posts)

Operator rule: the first week should look organized and confident.

SECTION 11: THE “FIRST BATCH FAST” RULE

Retention is earned when the client sees output quickly.

Operator standard: deliver the first batch within 3–5 business days after assets and intake are complete (adjust to your offer).

Why it matters:

  • clients are excited in week 1
  • if you go silent for 10 days, they feel regret
  • fast delivery builds trust instantly

Operator rule: speed early builds retention later.

SECTION 12: ONBOARDING FAILURES (AND FIXES)

Failure 1: client doesn’t send footage
Fix: define upload deadlines; if they don’t upload, delivery shifts.

Failure 2: too many decision-makers
Fix: one approver rule.

Failure 3: endless revisions
Fix: revision limit and clear definition of a re-edit.

Failure 4: unclear brand voice
Fix: intake + style guide + reference videos.

Failure 5: no delivery cadence
Fix: calendar schedule set during onboarding.

Failure 6: posting confusion
Fix: clearly assign posting responsibility.

Operator rule: onboarding failures are process failures. Fix process, not emotions.

SECTION 13: ONBOARDING PACKET (WHAT YOU SHOULD SEND)

You should have a simple onboarding packet (PDF or Notion) that includes:

  • scope summary
  • deliverables and cadence
  • folder link + upload instructions
  • approval and revision rules
  • contact channel and response expectations
  • next delivery date
  • “what we need from you” checklist

Operator rule: professional packet reduces questions and builds trust.

SECTION 14: COMMUNICATION RULES (STOP CLIENT CHAOS)

Set boundaries:

  • where they message you (one channel)
  • response times (e.g., same day during business hours)
  • urgent requests policy (rush fees or next batch)
  • how feedback should be sent (one message, timestamps)

Operator rule: communication boundaries protect production.

SECTION 15: ASSIGNMENTS

Assignment 1: Build your intake form
Write the questions for brand, offer, audience, style references, logistics.

Assignment 2: Build your folder template
Create the standard folder structure for every client.

Assignment 3: Write your onboarding packet
Scope, cadence, approvals, revisions, what you need from them.

Assignment 4: Create your “first 2-week plan” template
Content pillars + hook list + posting cadence.

Assignment 5: Run a mock onboarding
Pretend you just closed a client and walk through the 7 steps. Time yourself.

SECTION 16: QUIZ

  1. What is the 7-step onboarding flow?
  2. What must scope confirmation include?
  3. What must the intake form collect to prevent revision loops?
  4. What is your folder structure and why does it matter?
  5. What are your approval and revision rules?
  6. Why is “first batch fast” important for retention?
  7. What is included in your onboarding packet?

CLOSING

Onboarding is your quality control. Lock scope, collect assets, establish a style guide, set approvals and revisions, and deliver the first batch fast. That’s how you keep clients, protect your schedule, and scale without chaos.

CONTENT AGENCY PATH · LESSON 8: Production Workflow

Content pipeline, approvals, deadlines

Operator Standard

Do not just read the lesson. Pull the rule, run the rep, and keep proof that the rep happened.

Action Steps
  1. Read the lesson carefully and pull the rules that apply to your business or workflow right now.
  2. Turn those rules into one concrete action you can complete this week.
  3. Use the assignment, quick start, or deliverable section as the work product for this lesson.
  4. Review mistakes, update your system, and then move forward.

  • Your notes on what worked, what failed, and what you will change on the next rep.
  • A quiz score or completion mark so you can track progress inside the member area.

Back to Content Agency PathPrevious lessonNext lesson
Content pipeline, approvals, deadlines

PURPOSE OF THIS LESSON

This is where your agency becomes a machine.

Most agencies don’t fail because they can’t edit. They fail because production is chaotic: files everywhere, unclear deadlines, endless revisions, no version control, clients approving late, and editors working from guesswork.

Operators build a production pipeline. It’s the same every week, for every client: inputs → production → review → revisions → final → delivery → reporting. When the pipeline is clean, you can scale volume, hire editors, and keep quality stable.

This lesson gives you a full production workflow with:

  • a weekly cadence that prevents last-minute panic
  • file organization and naming rules
  • a content calendar and batching system
  • draft review and approval flow
  • revision control
  • deadline management
  • quality checks so your output stays professional

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of Lesson 8, the student will be able to:

  1. Run a weekly content pipeline that delivers on time consistently.
  2. Batch production to reduce context switching and editor mistakes.
  3. Set up folder structure and naming conventions for version control.
  4. Use an approvals system that prevents endless back-and-forth.
  5. Manage revisions with clear rules and deadlines.
  6. Maintain quality through checklists and style guides.
  7. Track production metrics (throughput, revision rate, on-time delivery).

SECTION 1: THE OPERATOR PRODUCTION PRINCIPLE

Production is not creativity. Production is logistics.

Your job is to turn raw assets into finished deliverables on schedule. If production is inconsistent, retention dies.

Operator rule: the workflow is the product.

SECTION 2: THE CORE PIPELINE (THE ONLY PIPELINE YOU NEED)

Every client runs the same pipeline:

  1. Input received (raw footage)
  2. Clip selection + hook planning
  3. Draft edit (V1)
  4. Internal QA (checklist)
  5. Client review/approval window
  6. Revisions (V2)
  7. Final export + deliver
  8. Posting handoff (or scheduling)
  9. ArchI’ve + metrics log

Operator rule: if you skip steps, you get rework and complaints.

SECTION 3: THE WEEKLY CADENCE (SCHEDULE THAT PREVENTS CHAOS)

Pick a weekly cadence and enforce it.

Example cadence (high-performing and simple):

  • Sunday: client uploads footage by 8pm
  • Monday: hook plan + clip selection
  • Tuesday: drafts delivered (V1)
  • Wednesday: client approvals due by noon
  • Thursday: revisions + finals delivered
  • Friday: buffer day + posting support + reporting notes

AlternatI’ve cadence (if you deliver 3x/week):

  • Monday/Wednesday/Friday deliveries

But you still need approval windows and revision deadlines.

Operator rule: cadence gives you leverage. Without cadence, clients control your week.

SECTION 4: INPUT RULES (NO INPUT, NO OUTPUT)

Your pipeline depends on input discipline.

Input rules to enforce:

  • upload deadline each week
  • minimum footage requirements (“X minutes of raw footage” or “1 long video”)
  • if footage is late, delivery shifts
  • if footage is missing, you can only repurpose existing library

Client message template (calm):
“Reminder: footage is due by Sunday night so we can deliver your batch Tuesday. If it comes in late, delivery shifts to the next cycle.”

Operator rule: you don’t punish clients—you operate by schedule.

SECTION 5: FILE SYSTEM + VERSION CONTROL (STOP LOSING TIME)

Chaos files = chaos delivery.

Standard folder structure per client:
/Client_Name
/01_Raw
/02_Clips_Selected
/03_Drafts_V1
/04_Revisions_V2
/05_Final
/06_Captions
/07_Style_Guide
/08_Reports

Naming convention (simple and powerful):
Client_Topic_Date_Version
Example:
MedSpa_BotoxMyths_2026-03-19_V1.mp4
MedSpa_BotoxMyths_2026-03-19_V2.mp4
MedSpa_BotoxMyths_2026-03-19_FINAL.mp4

Operator rule: naming conventions prevent “which file is the final?” disasters.

SECTION 6: BATCHING (HOW TO PRODUCE MORE WITHOUT BURNING OUT)

Batching reduces context switching.

Batching method:

  1. Hook planning for the whole week (all clips)
  2. Rough cuts for the whole batch
  3. Caption pass for the whole batch
  4. Sound/design pass for the whole batch
  5. QA for the whole batch
  6. Export and deliver

Why it works:

  • you do the same mental task in a row
  • faster and more consistent
  • less “relearn” time per client

Operator rule: batching is how agencies scale.

SECTION 7: HOOK PLANNING (THE FIRST 2 SECONDS RULE)

Before editing, you plan hooks.

Hook planning process:

  • choose 6–12 hook angles per week per client
  • map each clip to a pillar (education, proof, story, offer)
  • write a one-line hook for each video

This prevents random edits and makes performance better.

Operator rule: hook planning is production planning, not creativity.

SECTION 8: INTERNAL QA CHECKLIST (QUALITY CONTROL BEFORE CLIENT SEES IT)

Never send raw drafts without QA. Clients judge you by what you send.

QA checklist (short-form):

  • hook is clear in first 1–2 seconds
  • pacing is tight (dead air removed)
  • captions accurate (spelling, grammar)
  • brand tone matched (no weird slang if not brand)
  • video is correctly formatted (9:16, safe margins)
  • audio levels acceptable (no harsh peaks)
  • CTA included (if required)
  • no sensitive/copyright issues (reasonable check)
  • file name correct and versioned

Operator rule: QA prevents revision loops and protects reputation.

SECTION 9: CLIENT REVIEW SYSTEM (APPROVALS WITHOUT CHAOS)

Clients must review drafts within a window. Otherwise production stalls.

Approval rules:

  • client has 24–72 hours to approve (depends on your offer)
  • feedback must be consolidated (one person)
  • feedback must be timestamped
  • silence by deadline = auto-approve OR delivery shifts (choose one policy and state it)

Feedback format example:
“Video 1: 0:03 change ‘X’ to ‘Y’
Video 2: remove clip at 0:10
Video 3: keep as is”

Operator rule: unstructured feedback creates infinite revisions.

SECTION 10: REVISION CONTROL (HOW TO STOP ENDLESS EDITS)

Revision rules you enforce:

  • 1 revision per video included (or 2 rounds per batch)
  • “new concept” requests are a new video, not a revision
  • re-edits are billed or moved to next cycle
  • revision requests must arrI’ve by a deadline to keep schedule

Revision policy script:
“Happy to adjust. Your package includes [X] revision(s). If you want a full re-angle or new concept, we can treat that as an additional edit or roll it into next week’s batch.”

Operator rule: revision control keeps your margins alive.

SECTION 11: DEADLINES (THE AGENCY IS A CALENDAR BUSINESS)

You need three deadlines:

  1. Footage deadline
  2. Approval deadline
  3. Final delivery deadline

If any of those slips, the entire week slides. That’s why you enforce deadlines.

Operator rule: deadlines protect both client and agency.

SECTION 12: COMMUNICATION RULES (PRODUCTION PROTECTION)

Communication should not interrupt production constantly.

Rules:

  • one channel for requests
  • one daily check-in time for messages (optional)
  • urgent requests have a rush fee or next-cycle rule
  • no random “can you do this today” without schedule impact

Operator rule: constant interruptions destroy throughput.

SECTION 13: POSTING HANDOFF (WHO POSTS WHAT)

Decide this in onboarding and enforce it.

If client posts:

  • you deliver finals + captions + posting schedule suggestion
  • client posts and tags as appropriate

If agency posts:

  • you need access and a posting SOP
  • you need a content calendar
  • you need approval on caption/CTA rules

Operator rule: unclear posting responsibility creates missed content and blame.

SECTION 14: METRICS FOR PRODUCTION (HOW YOU KNOW IF YOU’RE SCALING)

Track weekly:

  • videos delivered
  • on-time delivery rate
  • average revision count per video
  • revision causes (captions wrong vs “style preference”)
  • time per video (rough estimate)
  • client approval speed

Use these to improve:

  • high revisions = style guide unclear or QA weak
  • late approvals = approval rules not enforced
  • late delivery = batching broken or scope too big

Operator rule: production metrics show where the system leaks.

SECTION 15: COMMON WORKFLOW FAILURES (AND FIXES)

Failure 1: client doesn’t send footage
Fix: input deadline + fallback to repurpose library.

Failure 2: drafts delivered late
Fix: batch editing and protect production blocks.

Failure 3: too many revisions
Fix: QA checklist + revision limits + better intake.

Failure 4: file chaos
Fix: folder structure + naming conventions.

Failure 5: approvals slow
Fix: one approver + approval window + “delivery shifts” policy.

Failure 6: too many clients too soon
Fix: cap clients based on capacity and maintain quality.

Operator rule: most workflow failures are discipline failures, not talent failures.

SECTION 16: THE “WEEKLY PRODUCTION RUN” (COPY/PASTE CHECKLIST)

Weekly Production Run

  1. Monday: hook plan + clip selection
  2. Tuesday: draft batch (V1) + internal QA
  3. Tuesday: deliver drafts to client
  4. Wednesday: collect consolidated feedback by deadline
  5. Thursday: revisions (V2) + finals + deliver
  6. Friday: buffer fixes + schedule/post handoff + update metrics

Operator rule: run this every week and your agency becomes predictable.

SECTION 17: ASSIGNMENTS

Assignment 1: Build your workflow doc
Write your pipeline steps and weekly cadence.

Assignment 2: Build folder structure + naming rules
Create a template you duplicate per client.

Assignment 3: Create QA checklist
Keep it short and mandatory.

Assignment 4: Create approval + revision rules
Write your approval window and revision policy.

Assignment 5: Run a mock production week
Take one piece of footage and run it through the pipeline as if it’s a client.

SECTION 18: QUIZ

  1. What are the 9 steps of the content pipeline?
  2. Why does weekly cadence prevent chaos?
  3. What are the three deadlines and why do they matter?
  4. What is batching and why does it increase output?
  5. What belongs on an internal QA checklist?
  6. How do you structure client feedback to avoid revision loops?
  7. What production metrics tell you where the system is leaking?

CLOSING

Production workflow is the agency. A clean pipeline, clear deadlines, version control, batching, QA, and disciplined approvals make delivery predictable. Predictable delivery creates retention—and retention is the real money.

LESSON 9: Creative that Performs
Operator Training Flow
Study it. Execute it. Save proof.
